note_data.title: Reproduction
keyPoints: **Sexual reproduction** involves the fusion of male and female gametes to produce offspring that are genetically different from parents
**Asexual reproduction** involves only one parent and produces offspring that are genetically identical to the parent (clones)
The flower is the **reproductive organ** of flowering plants containing male parts (stamens) and female parts (carpels)
**Pollination** is the transfer of pollen grains from the anther to the stigma of the same species
**Self-pollination** occurs when pollen is transferred within the same flower or between flowers on the same plant
**Cross-pollination** occurs when pollen is transferred between flowers on different plants of the same species
**Wind-pollinated flowers** have small, dull petals, long feathery stigmas, and produce large amounts of light pollen
**Insect-pollinated flowers** have large, brightly coloured petals, sticky pollen, and often produce nectar
**Fertilisation** occurs when the male gamete (in pollen tube) fuses with the female gamete (ovum) in the ovule

definitions.definition: A process involving the fusion of male and female gametes to produce offspring that are genetically different from the parents
A process involving only one parent that produces offspring genetically identical to the parent
The transfer of pollen grains from the anther to the stigma of the same species
The fusion of male and female gametes
The process by which a seed begins to grow into a new plant

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give similarities AND differences between two things
Give detailed account of structure or process in logical sequence

modelAnswer.question: Explain two advantages of cross-pollination over self-pollination.
modelAnswer.answer: Cross-pollination produces genetic variation in offspring because gametes from different plants combine. This increases the chance of survival in changing environmental conditions because some offspring may have favourable characteristics. Cross-pollination reduces the risk of harmful recessive alleles being expressed because genes from different plants are combined.
markBreakdown: Mark 2: Because gametes from different plants combine
Mark 3: Increases survival chances in changing conditions
Mark 4: Reduces expression of harmful recessive alleles

commonMistakes: Never say 'pollination is reproduction' — pollination is transfer of pollen, fertilisation is fusion of gametes
Students often confuse wind and insect pollinated features — remember wind flowers are small and dull, insect flowers are large and bright
Don't say 'asexual reproduction involves no gametes' — say 'involves only one parent'
Never describe germination as 'fertilisation' — germination is seed growth, fertilisation is gamete fusion
Students forget that cross-pollination must be between different plants of the SAME species
examinerTips: Flower structure diagrams appear frequently in Paper 1 and Paper 3 - learn all parts
Compare questions on sexual vs asexual reproduction are very common in Paper 2
Always mention 'genetic variation' when explaining advantages of sexual reproduction
For pollination adaptations, give specific examples like sticky vs powdery pollen
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Structure of a complete flower showing sepals, petals, stamens (anther and filament), and carpel (stigma, style, ovary with ovules). Essential for Paper 1 and Paper 3 practical examinations.
